title: SEGMENT 7 — Accelerated approval pathways — the fast lanes through the gauntlet

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Accelerated approval pathways — the fast lanes through the gauntlet
Before we walk into the clinical trial segments, I want to stop and tell you about a set of regulatory mechanisms that are quietly transformative for how gene therapies move through the system.

These are the accelerated pathways, sometimes called the expedited programs, and they are designed to speed up the development and review of drugs that target serious or life-threatening conditions with significant unmet medical need.
Gene therapies, by their nature, almost always meet those criteria, so gene therapy programs disproportionately qualify for one or more of these designations.
There are four major designations in the US system you need to know.
Fast Track.
Breakthrough Therapy.
Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy — RMAT.
Priority Review.
Each one provides slightly different benefits.
Each one has slightly different qualification criteria.

And clever sponsors stack them — applying for Fast Track first, then Breakthrough or RMAT once they have early clinical data, then Priority Review at the time of the BLA — to compress the timeline as much as possible.
Fast Track is the oldest of the four.
It was created in the late 1990s.
The criteria are broad: a drug for a serious condition with the potential to address unmet medical need can qualify.

The benefits include more frequent communication with the agency, eligibility for rolling review — meaning you can submit your BLA in pieces as they are ready rather than all at once — and accelerated review timelines.
Most gene therapies qualify for Fast Track, and most apply for it early, sometimes at or even before the IND stage.
Breakthrough Therapy is more demanding.
Created in 2012, it requires that you have preliminary clinical evidence suggesting your drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on a clinically significant endpoint.
That means you generally can't apply for Breakthrough until you have at least some Phase 1 data showing real efficacy.
The benefits are bigger than Fast Track: intensive guidance from FDA on efficient drug development, organizational commitment of senior reviewers, and rolling review.
Breakthrough designation is, in my experience talking to people in the field, the single most useful accelerated designation because of the dedicated FDA attention it brings.
You essentially get a more engaged review team.
RMAT — Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy — is the newest and most gene-therapy-specific.

It was created by the 21st Century Cures Act in 2016, and it was designed explicitly to accommodate the unusual features of cell and gene therapy development — small patient populations, durable single-dose effects, the difficulty of running traditional Phase 3 trials.

To qualify for RMAT, your product has to be a regenerative medicine therapy — which includes cell therapies, gene therapies, and tissue-engineered products — for a serious condition, and you need preliminary clinical evidence indicating potential to address unmet need.

The benefits are similar to Breakthrough — intensive FDA guidance, eligibility for rolling and Priority Review — but with the addition of explicit allowance for using surrogate or intermediate endpoints, post-approval studies in lieu of pre-approval ones, and other accommodations that recognize that gene therapy doesn't fit the small-molecule template.
For a gene therapy program, RMAT is usually the right ask.
Priority Review is different from the other three.
It is not a development-phase designation; it is a review-phase designation.
When you file your BLA, you can request Priority Review, which shortens the FDA's target review timeline from ten months to six months.
The agency grants Priority Review to applications that, if approved, would provide significant improvement in safety or effectiveness of treatment for a serious condition.
Many gene therapies, by the time they reach BLA, easily qualify.
Here is the analogy.
The accelerated pathways are the fast lanes.
Imagine the regulatory gauntlet as a highway, and most drugs travel in the regular lanes — they sit in traffic, they wait their turn, they cover the same distance as everyone else.
The accelerated pathways are dedicated lanes on the side of that highway, with fewer cars, fewer stops, and direct on-ramps to the agency's attention.
If you qualify, you can shave months or even years off the development and review timeline.
For a small patient population dying of a fatal disease, those months matter enormously.
I'll mention the EU side briefly.
The European equivalent of the US accelerated pathways is called PRIME — Priority Medicines — administered by EMA.
PRIME provides enhanced interaction with the agency and is broadly comparable to Breakthrough Therapy in concept.
Separately, the EU has an adaptive licensing approach for advanced therapy medicinal products, run through CAT, that allows for staged approvals based on accumulating evidence.
The mechanics differ in detail, but the underlying philosophy — speeding up access for serious diseases with unmet need — is the same on both sides of the Atlantic.
One important caveat.
Accelerated pathways do not lower the bar for approval.
They do not change the safety or efficacy standards your drug ultimately has to meet.
They change the speed and the structure of how you get there.
A drug that gets Breakthrough designation and then fails in Phase 3 has still failed.
The fast lane gets you to the gate faster.
It does not change what the gate requires.
